Complete Revision Notes

Machine
Learning

Intuition-first approach — every concept explained with why before how. Linear Regression end-to-end.

Sections
10
Topic
Linear Regression
Level
Foundations
01

What is Machine Learning?

Machine Learning (ML) is a branch of Computer Science where we teach computers to learn patterns from data and make decisions or predictions — without being explicitly programmed for each task.
Intuition

Traditional programming: you write the rules → computer applies them. ML: you give examples → computer figures out the rules itself. Like teaching a child what a cat is by showing many photos, not by listing every rule about cats.

Key difference: In ML, the model discovers the rules (hypothesis) itself from data

The ML Flow

Once trained, the model acts like a function: give it new input → it predicts output using the patterns it learned.

Training happens once → model is reused for all new predictions

02

Types of Machine Learning

Three main paradigms of Machine Learning

Supervised Learning
  • Data is labelled — every input has a known correct output
  • Model learns mapping: X → Y
  • Two types: Classification & Regression
Spam detectionHouse prices
Unsupervised Learning
  • Data is unlabelled — no correct answers given
  • Model discovers hidden patterns
  • Main task: Clustering
Customer segmentsAnomaly detection
Reinforcement Learning
  • Agent interacts with environment
  • Gets rewards (good) or penalties (bad)
  • Learns optimal strategy over time
AlphaGoSelf-driving
03

Supervised ML — Features & Labels

In supervised ML, your dataset has features (X) — the inputs — and a label (Y) — the output you want to predict. The model learns the function X → Y.

Supervised learning dataset: each row is one training example. Model learns to predict Y from X.

Classification — Category Output

Output is one of several discrete classes.

  • Binary (2 classes): Spam / Not spam, Malignant / Benign
  • Multi-class (3+ exclusive): Cat / Dog / Rabbit
  • Multi-label (multiple tags): Movie → Action AND Funny
Why?

Use classification when the answer is a bucket, not a number. "Will this customer churn?" = Yes/No = Classification.

Regression — Continuous Output

Output is a continuous number on a scale.

  • House price prediction
  • Stock price tomorrow
  • Age estimation from image
  • Insurance charges
Why?

Use regression when the answer is a number. "How much will this house sell for?" = ₹X = Regression.

04

Steps for Building an ML Model

ML pipeline — each step feeds into the next; evaluation may loop back to tuning

  • Data Pre-processing + Feature Engineering Clean data, handle missing values, encode categorical variables (Label Encoding, One-Hot), normalise/scale features. Rule: No → 0, Yes → 1. This step directly impacts model quality.
  • Select Model Choose algorithm based on problem type (Classification? Regression?), data size, interpretability needs. Linear Regression for continuous output, Logistic for binary classification, etc.
  • Train / Test (model.fit) Split data: typically 80% training, 20% testing. Train on training set. Evaluate on test set (unseen data) to check real-world performance.
  • Underfit / Overfit Analysis Underfit: model too simple, high error on both train and test (high bias). Overfit: model memorised training data, low train error but high test error (high variance). Goal: find the sweet spot.
05

Scikit-Learn (sklearn)

Scikit-Learn is Python's most popular ML library. It provides ready-to-use implementations of all major ML algorithms with a consistent API: model.fit(X, y) to train, model.predict(X) to predict.
Why Scikit-Learn?
  • Well documented, easy to learn
  • Works seamlessly with NumPy & Pandas
  • Used by professionals and beginners
  • Consistent API across all algorithms
Setup
python -m venv sklearn-env
# Activate (Windows)
sklearn-env\Scripts\activate
# Install
pip install -U scikit-learn

Algorithms at a Glance

AlgorithmTypeKey UseOutput
Linear RegressionRegressionPredict continuous valuesNumber
Logistic RegressionClassificationBinary / multi-classClass
KNNBothInstance-based, k nearestClass/Number
Decision TreesBothRule-based, interpretableClass/Number
SVMBothMax-margin hyperplaneClass/Number
Naïve BayesClassificationProbabilistic, textClass
06

Linear Regression — Deep Dive

Linear Regression finds the single best-fit straight line through your data points that minimises prediction errors. Used for predicting continuous numerical outputs.
Intuition first

Imagine plotting study hours (X) vs exam score (Y). Some points are scattered. Linear Regression finds the ONE line that is "closest" to all points simultaneously — so you can predict score for any new study hour value.

Best-fit line has POSITIVE slope: as x (hours) increases, y (score) increases. Residual = vertical gap between actual point and line.

Hypothesis Function (Full Form)

For multiple features (Multiple Linear Regression):

Hypothesis Function
hθ(x) = θ₀ + θ₁x₁ + θ₂x₂ + ... + θₙxₙ
hθ(x)The model's prediction (same as ŷ). Reads as "h sub theta of x"
θ₀ (theta-0)Bias / Intercept — the y-intercept. Value of prediction when all x = 0. It's the baseline.
θ₁…θₙWeights / Coefficients — how much each feature contributes. Larger θ₁ = feature x₁ matters more.
x₁…xₙFeatures — your input variables (age, BMI, salary, etc.)
Why these symbols?

θ (theta) are the parameters the model learns. During training, gradient descent adjusts these θ values until predictions are as accurate as possible. After training, θ values are fixed and used for all new predictions.

07

Cost Function — Measuring Error

Cost Function J(θ) measures how wrong the model's predictions are overall. It takes all the prediction errors, squares them, and averages. Lower J(θ) = better model. Goal of training = minimise J(θ).
Why do we need it?

The model has infinite possible lines it could draw. The cost function gives us a single number to judge "how bad is this line?" — so we can compare lines and find the best one. Without J(θ), we'd have no way to know if θ₁=2 is better than θ₁=3.

Mean Squared Error (MSE)

Cost Function — MSE
J(θ) = 1/(2m) · Σᵢ (hθ(xⁱ) − yⁱ)²
J(θ)Total cost — the number we want to minimise
mNumber of training examples
hθ(xⁱ)Model's prediction for example i
yⁱActual true label for example i
(hθ − y)²Squared error — penalises large errors more than small ones
1/(2m)1/m = average; extra ÷2 makes derivative cleaner (cancels the 2 from squaring)
◆
Why square the error? If you just summed (hθ − y), positive and negative errors would cancel out (−5 + 5 = 0, looks perfect but isn't). Squaring makes all errors positive AND punishes big errors extra hard: error of 10 becomes 100, but error of 2 becomes only 4.

Cost function J(θ) is a BOWL opening upward — minimum is at the BOTTOM. Our goal is to reach that bottom point.

✓
Key insight: For every combination of (θ₀, θ₁), there's one J(θ) value. The cost function is like a landscape of hills and valleys. We want to find the lowest valley = minimum J(θ) = best model.
08

Gradient Descent — Finding the Minimum

Gradient Descent is an iterative optimisation algorithm that minimises the cost function J(θ) by repeatedly adjusting the parameters θ in the direction of steepest descent (downhill).
Real-world Intuition

Imagine you're blindfolded on a hilly mountain and want to reach the lowest valley. Your strategy: feel the slope under your feet → take a step in whichever direction goes downhill → repeat. Eventually you'll reach the bottom. That's exactly what gradient descent does with J(θ).

CORRECT: Left side of bowl has NEGATIVE slope, right side has POSITIVE slope. Subtracting the slope always moves θ toward the minimum.

The Update Rule — Explained Symbol by Symbol

Gradient Descent Update Rule (repeat for all k simultaneously)
θₖ := θₖ − α · ∂J(θ)/∂θₖ
θₖ (new)Updated parameter value after this step
θₖ (old)Current parameter value before update
α (alpha)Learning rate — how big a step to take. Hyperparameter, set by you, typically 0.01–0.1
∂J(θ)/∂θₖGradient (partial derivative) — the slope of the cost function at the current θ. Tells direction of steepest climb.
Minus signWe subtract the gradient because we want to go opposite to it (downhill, not uphill)

Learning Rate α — The Critical Hyperparameter

Interactive: See effect of different learning rates
0.30
⚠
α too small
Takes tiny steps. Training is painfully slow. May need thousands of iterations to converge.
✓
α just right
Efficient convergence. Steps are well-sized. Reaches minimum smoothly in few iterations.
✕
α too large
Overshoots the minimum. Bounces back and forth. May diverge — cost INCREASES instead of decreasing.

Step-by-Step Gradient Descent Algorithm

  • Initialise θ₀ = 0, θ₁ = 0Start parameters at zero (or small random values). The algorithm will learn the correct values.
  • Make predictions using hθ(x)For each training example, compute hθ(xⁱ) = θ₀ + θ₁x₁ᵢ using current θ values.
  • Calculate cost J(θ)Compute J(θ) = (1/2m)·Σ(hθ(xⁱ)−yⁱ)². This tells us how wrong we currently are.
  • Compute gradient ∂J/∂θCalculate partial derivative of J with respect to each θₖ. This is the slope — tells us which direction to step.
  • Update all θ simultaneouslyθₖ := θₖ − α·∂J/∂θₖ for all k. Update ALL parameters at once, not one at a time.
  • Repeat until convergenceKeep looping steps 2–5 until J(θ) stops decreasing meaningfully. This is the "convergence theorem" — for properly chosen α, guaranteed to reach minimum.

Gradient descent is a loop: predict → measure error → find slope → update parameters → repeat

09

Implementation — Insurance Dataset

◆
We predict insurance charges from features: age, sex, BMI, smoker status, region. This is a regression problem — predicting a continuous numeric output.

Step 1: Load & Explore Data

import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

# Load dataset
insurance_data = pd.read_csv("insurance.csv")
insurance_data.head()

# Visualise relationship between BMI and charges
sns.scatterplot(
    x=insurance_data["bmi"],
    y=insurance_data["charges"],
    hue=insurance_data["smoker"]  # color by smoker
)

Step 2: Feature Engineering (Encode Categorical)

# Features (X) = everything except charges and region
X = insurance_data.drop(columns=["charges", "region"])
y = insurance_data["charges"]

# Encode: convert text categories to numbers
# ML models need numbers, not "male"/"female" strings
X["sex"]    = X["sex"].map({"female": 1, "male": 0})
X["smoker"] = X["smoker"].map({"yes": 1, "no": 0})

Step 3: Train–Test Split

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# Split: 80% train, 20% test, random_state for reproducibility
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y,
    test_size=0.2,        # 20% for testing
    random_state=42      # reproducible split
)

X_train.head()    # check training features
X_test.head()     # check test features

Step 4: Train the Model

from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

model = LinearRegression()
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
# model.fit() runs gradient descent internally
# After fit, model has learned θ₀, θ₁...θₙ

Step 5: Predict

# Predict on test set (unseen data)
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)
# y_pred = hθ(X_test) = θ₀ + θ₁x₁ + θ₂x₂ + ...
10

Evaluation Metrics

After predicting, we need to measure how good our model is. Different metrics highlight different aspects of error.

Three error metrics — use RMSE for reporting (same units as target), MSE for training (differentiable)

R² Score — Coefficient of Determination

R² measures what fraction of the variance in Y your model explains. Range: 0 to 1. R²=1 = perfect predictions. R²=0 = model is no better than just predicting the mean.
R-Squared Formula
R² = 1 − Σ(yᵢ − ŷᵢ)² / Σ(yᵢ − ȳ)²
Σ(yᵢ − ŷᵢ)²Residual SS — sum of squared errors from our MODEL. How wrong our model is.
Σ(yᵢ − ȳ)²Total SS — sum of squared errors from the MEAN baseline. If we just predicted the average every time.
R² = 1Model residual = 0 → perfect fit. Every prediction is exact.
R² = 0Model is as good as just predicting the mean — learned nothing.
R² < 0Model is WORSE than predicting the mean — very bad model.
Intuition

R² = "What percentage of the variation in Y did our model successfully explain?" R²=0.85 means the model explains 85% of why charges vary between customers. The remaining 15% is unexplained (other factors).

Adjusted R² — Penalises Useless Features

⚠
Problem with R²: Adding more features always increases R², even if the feature is useless (random noise). So R² can be gamed by just adding more columns.
Adjusted R-Squared
Adj. R² = 1 − (1−R²) · (n−1) / (n−p−1)
nNumber of rows (training examples)
pNumber of features (independent variables)
R²Regular R-squared score
When p ↑(n−p−1) decreases → penalty increases → Adj. R² decreases if the new feature didn't actually help

Implementation

from sklearn.metrics import r2_score
import numpy as np

# Basic metrics
mae  = np.mean(np.abs(y_test - y_pred))
mse  = np.mean((y_test - y_pred)**2)
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
r2   = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Adjusted R²
n = X_test.shape[0]    # number of rows
p = X_test.shape[1]    # number of features
adjusted_r2 = 1 - ((1 - r2) * (n - 1) / (n - p - 1))

print(f"MAE:          {mae:.2f}")
print(f"MSE:          {mse:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE:         {rmse:.2f}")
print(f"R²:           {r2:.4f}")
print(f"Adjusted R²:  {adjusted_r2:.4f}")

Complete sklearn pipeline for Linear Regression
